explanation:
The natural language requirement states that the system should begin monitoring and reporting health parameters only after two predecessor conditions are both met: the self-test is completed successfully, and the configurations are properly loaded. Mapping to the atomic propositions, 'selfTestPass' indicates the self-test has passed, and 'initDone' indicates loading configurations is done. 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' indicate starting monitoring and reporting of health parameters, respectively. The correct LTL property must enforce that both 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' do not occur until after BOTH 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' have held at least once, and that EVENTUALLY once both have held, 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' will occur.

To capture the 'after both, do X' semantics: A common LTL idiom is to say:
- it is always the case that (if BOTH preconditions have NOT yet occurred), then the postcondition cannot occur: ( !(selfTestPass & initDone) ) U (startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams )
But here, we want even more precise semantics: the system shall start monitoring and reporting health parameters ONCE both selfTestPass and initDone have occurred. Since there is no numerical comparison, we ensure that 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' happen AFTER the first time both preconditions hold, and DO NOT happen before that.

A precise translation:
( !(selfTestPass & initDone) ) U (startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams)

However, this alone doesn't guarantee that startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams actually happen eventually after both selfTestPass and initDone. So, we can add:
G( (selfTestPass & initDone) -> F(startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams) )

Combining, the property is:
G( ( !(selfTestPass & initDone) ) -> !(startMonitoring | startReportingHealthParams)) & G( (selfTestPass & initDone) -> F(startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams) )

This LTL property best captures the described requirement using only the available atomic propositions and LTL operators. Previous possible approaches might only use U or F, but would risk allowing reporting before both preconditions, or not ensure that reporting eventually happens after the preconditions are met.
output_LTL: G( (!(selfTestPass & initDone)) -> !(startMonitoring | startReportingHealthParams) ) & G( (selfTestPass & initDone) -> F(startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams) )

explanation:
The input requirement states that after the self-test passes and the configurations are loaded ("initDone"), the system must start monitoring and reporting health parameters. Since we have atomic propositions 'selfTestPass' (self-test passes), 'initDone' (configurations loaded and checking done), 'startMonitoring' (system starts monitoring), and 'startReportingHealthParams' (system starts reporting health parameters), the most direct LTL formula is an 'until' construct that ensures monitoring and reporting begins only after both preconditions are met.

We capture this sequential dependency using the following: globally, whenever both 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' hold (possibly at the same time), it is required that eventually both 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' become true. This ensures monitoring and reporting only follow successful self-test and initialization, as per the natural language statement.

Past LTLs (if any) might not have adequately enforced the requirement that both selfTest and initialization must succeed before monitoring and reporting can start. This translation makes this dependency explicit.

The property states that once the self-test has been passed successfully ('selfTestPass') and configurations have been loaded and the internal checking is done ('initDone'), then it is required that eventually the system starts monitoring and reporting health parameters ('startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams'). This captures the natural language requirement that only after these successful completions should the system begin health reporting. The use of the 'G' (globally) operator ensures this relationship holds throughout execution. No alternative LTL properties were previously given for comparison.

The natural language statement specifies a sequence: first, the self-test must be completed successfully (indicated by 'selfTestPass'), and configurations must be loaded properly (indicated by 'initDone'), and only after both of these have occurred, the system should start monitoring (indicated by 'startMonitoring') and reporting health parameters (indicated by 'startReportingHealthParams'). The correct LTL property expresses that globally, it is always the case that if 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' both become true, then (at some future point after this) 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' must both occur. This ensures the required sequence and dependency. The translation assumes that 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' both occur after the prerequisites are satisfied, which is the strongest way to capture the described trigger-action semantics.

The requirement states: Once the self-test has been completed successfully (selfTestPass) AND configurations have been loaded properly (initDone), THEN the system shall start monitoring (startMonitoring) AND reporting health parameters (startReportingHealthParams). This is a sequential, conditional property: after both selfTestPass and initDone have occurred, startMonitoring and startReportingHealthParams must occur. In LTL, this is typically captured by using the 'until' operator to enforce that until both conditions are met, the action cannot happen, and then requiring that once both have occurred, the postconditions eventually occur. This translation uses: (!((selfTestPass & initDone)) U (selfTestPass & initDone)), which waits until both conditions happen, then enforces that eventually afterward, startMonitoring and startReportingHealthParams must occur (F(startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams)). This property does not use any numeric comparison or non-atomic propositions, as required.

The natural language requirement states that once the self-test has passed (selfTestPass) and the system configurations have been loaded (initDone), the system shall start monitoring (startMonitoring) and reporting health parameters (startReportingHealthParams). This means both preconditions (selfTestPass and initDone) must be true before the monitoring/reporting can start, and only after that these actions are permitted. In LTL, this is best captured using a 'release' (V) operator for the safety part, which ensures startMonitoring and startReportingHealthParams do not occur until selfTestPass and initDone have both happened. Then, eventually, once these prerequisites are done, the system does start monitoring and reporting. The property G( ( !startMonitoring & !startReportingHealthParams ) V ( selfTestPass & initDone ) ) says that globally, before both selfTestPass and initDone are true, startMonitoring and startReportingHealthParams are not true. Furthermore, G( ( ( selfTestPass & initDone ) -> F ( startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams ) ) ) ensures that once both conditions are met, eventually both monitoring and reporting will occur. This also prevents the system from monitoring or reporting health parameters prematurely (before both preconditions).
output_LTL: G( ( !startMonitoring & !startReportingHealthParams ) V ( selfTestPass & initDone ) ) & G( ( ( selfTestPass & initDone ) -> F ( startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams ) ) )

explanation:
The natural language requirement states that the system shall start monitoring and reporting health parameters once (i.e., after) the self-test has completed successfully AND the configurations have been loaded properly. The most relevant atomic propositions are 'selfTestPass' (indicates self-test passed), 'initDone' (configurations have been loaded and checking terminated), 'startMonitoring' (system starts monitoring health parameters), and 'startReportingHealthParams' (system starts reporting health parameters).

The requirement is best modeled as: it is always the case that if (selfTestPass AND initDone) becomes true, then eventually both startMonitoring AND startReportingHealthParams will occur. To avoid any premature action, we also require that before both selfTestPass and initDone have occurred, neither startMonitoring nor startReportingHealthParams occur. This gives two parts:

1. Globally, ((selfTestPass & initDone) -> F (startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams)) — whenever both preconditions are satisfied, eventually the system will start both activities.

2. Globally, (!(selfTestPass & initDone) -> !(startMonitoring | startReportingHealthParams)) — before both preconditions, the system does not start either activity.

Putting together, we can express the core intended behavior as:
G( (selfTestPass & initDone) -> F (startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams) )

Previous LTL formulas might fail to tightly link the AND condition, may miss the prevention of early monitoring/reporting, or might not trigger on both conditions together. This one represents the required sequence and causality precisely.

The input natural language specifies that the system must only start monitoring and reporting health parameters once two conditions are satisfied: (1) the self-test has been successfully completed, and (2) the configurations have been properly loaded. In terms of atomic propositions, these two conditions correspond to 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone', while the task to be performed corresponds to 'startReportingHealthParams'. In LTL, this requirement means that globally, 'startReportingHealthParams' can only be true after both 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' have occurred. Using the 'release' (V) operator allows us to state that 'startReportingHealthParams' cannot occur before both 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone': (!startReportingHealthParams) V (selfTestPass & initDone). This captures the requirement that 'startReportingHealthParams' is forbidden from happening until both preconditions are satisfied. Previous LTL attempts that might just capture implication (e.g. (selfTestPass & initDone) -> F startReportingHealthParams) would not be strong enough because they would allow 'startReportingHealthParams' to occur before its preconditions. The release operator ensures the temporal order strictly.

The requirement states that the system must start monitoring and reporting health parameters only after both the self-test is successfully completed and configuration loading is done. The atomic propositions 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' respectively capture successful self-test completion and configuration loading. 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' represent the system beginning to monitor/report health parameters. The correct temporal relationship is that, globally, whenever both 'selfTestPass' and 'initDone' become true (possibly simultaneously), it must be the case that eventually (but not before) 'startMonitoring' and 'startReportingHealthParams' both hold. This property prohibits starting monitoring or reporting before both preconditions are satisfied and ensures they will eventually begin afterward. Thus, we encode it as: G ( (selfTestPass & initDone) -> F (startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams) ).

The requirement specifies that AFTER the self-test has completed successfully (represented by 'selfTestPass') AND the configurations have been loaded properly (represented by 'initDone'), the system shall (from that point onward) start monitoring ('startMonitoring') and reporting health parameters ('startReportingHealthParams'). We can model that by stating: Globally, if at any point selfTestPass and initDone both become true, then from that moment onwards, it is always the case that the system is monitoring AND reporting health parameters. This is best captured using the LTL formula: (G((selfTestPass & initDone) -> G(startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams)))

Previous LTL variants not using the strong implication from the condition point forward, or not conjoining the two necessary preconditions, would not correctly capture the order and permanence required by the original statement. For example, a formula like F(selfTestPass & initDone & startMonitoring & startReportingHealthParams) would simply require all of these happen at some point, which is not the same as ensuring reporting/monitoring continues after successful preconditions.
